task_completion.description: GeoGebra 3D Graphics view is open and includes a visual representation of the Horizon.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The GeoGebra application is open with the '3D Graphics' view active. The standard xy-plane (often shown as a gray grid) or a custom constructed plane is visible to represent the Horizon.

task_completion.description: An axis of rotation is constructed and angled at 47.6 degrees from the North Horizon.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A line or vector is visible in the 3D view, tilted relative to the xy-plane. The Algebra view on the left should show an angle of 47.6 degrees (or its complement, 42.4 degrees from the z-axis) defining this axis.

task_completion.description: A circular path representing the Equinox is created, centered at the origin.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A circle or arc is visible in the 3D view, centered at the origin (0,0,0) and oriented perpendicular to the tilted axis of rotation. The Algebra view should list this circle/curve.

task_completion.description: Summer and Winter Solstice sun paths are created with a +/- 23.44 degree offset.
task_completion.visual_evidence: Two additional circles or arcs are visible in the 3D view, parallel to the Equinox path. The Algebra view should show these paths offset by angles or distances corresponding to 23.44 degrees relative to the Equinox.

task_completion.description: The file is saved as 'seattle_sun_path.ggb' in the ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/ directory.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The GeoGebra window title displays 'seattle_sun_path.ggb', or a file manager/terminal window shows the file successfully saved in the '~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/' folder.

integrity.description: The agent used the GeoGebra GUI to construct the model.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots show the agent clicking tools, typing in the Algebra input bar, and interacting with the GeoGebra interface, rather than just running a script in the terminal to generate the file.

integrity.description: The 3D model is built using actual mathematical objects rather than imported static images.
integrity.visual_evidence: The Algebra view is populated with mathematical definitions (points, vectors, angles, circles/curves) that correspond to the visual 3D model.

integrity.description: The agent built the heliodon from scratch.
integrity.visual_evidence: The trajectory shows the step-by-step creation of the diagram. The agent does not simply open a downloaded or pre-existing '.ggb' file containing a completed sun path.

privileged_info_for_vlm: Seattle's latitude is 47.6° N, meaning the axis of rotation for the sun path must be angled at 47.6° from the horizontal plane (xy-plane), which corresponds to a co-latitude of 42.4° from the vertical (z-axis). The Earth's axial tilt (obliquity) is 23.44°, so the Summer and Winter Solstice paths must be offset by +23.44° and -23.44° respectively from the Equinox path. The expected output file is 'seattle_sun_path.ggb'.
